plan.contextChunk:
can use the equation of a line in homogeneous coordinates,
$\boldsymbol{l}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{p}=0$. From equation
(@eq-homography_generic), we have that
$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{p}'$. Replacing the last equality
into the equation of the line gives
$(\mathbf{H} ^{-\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{l})^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{p'}=0$,
which corresponds to the equation of the line for the projected points
$\mathbf{p}'$.

In a general homography, angles and lengths are not preserved.
Therefore, parallel lines might not remain parallel, and lines of
identical length might have different lengths after a homography.
@fig-illustration_homography2 shows how a planar grid gets transformed
after applying a homography.

![Homography. Colinear points remain colinear after transformation. Angles between lines are not preserved.](figures/homography/example_homography.png){width="70%" #fig-illustration_homography2}

Let's start discussing in which scenarios the coordinates of points
between to images are related by a homography.

### Camera Rotation

Consider the following three images shown in @fig-example_three_images.

:::{#fig-example_three_images layout-ncol="3"}
![](figures/homography/3_IMG_7883_small.jpg){width="52%"}

![](figures/homography/2_IMG_7884_small.jpg){width="30%"}

![](figures/homography/1_IMG_7885_small.jpg){width="30%"}

elements: horizontal arrow from left grid to right grid (indicates transformation)
all corresponding grid intersections between the two sides

interactions.teaches: Dragging any corner of the right grid interactively changes the homography, showing how lines bend and parallelism is lost but colinearity is preserved.
Hovering over any grid intersection on the left or right highlights the corresponding location on the other grid, reinforcing the idea of point correspondence under the homography.

interactions.label: Show line pairs
interactions.teaches: Toggles the display of connected lines between selected pairs of colinear points and their warped images, emphasizing how colinearity is maintained but angles and lengths are not.

demo_steps.title: Planar grid before homography
demo_steps.narration: This is a simple 4×4 square grid in its original, untransformed (axis-aligned) form. All lines are straight, parallel, and form right angles. This is our starting reference.

demo_steps.title: Apply a generic homography
demo_steps.narration: With the homography applied, the grid warps: lengths and angles are distorted, and parallel lines may no longer stay parallel. Notice that the overall planar structure remains, but much of the familiar geometry is lost.

demo_steps.title: Colinearity is preserved
demo_steps.narration: Turn on the line pairs overlay. Select two or more colinear points on the left: their connected images on the right still form a straight line, even as all the angles between grid lines are altered. This demonstrates a central property of homographies: colinear points remain colinear.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Flat, straight-on orthographic 2D view (no 3D tilt) to match original top-down diagram’s orientation; both grids appear planar and parallel to image borders.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant baseline is the square grid boundary, which runs parallel to the horizontal and vertical screen edges — both grids are shown as planar, with no indication of 3D perspective or tilt. (2) The grids’ construction aligns their axes with the screen, so the camera must be directly in front, broadside to the grids (azimuth 90 deg if using XY plane convention). (3) There is no foreshortening or vertical tilt visible — the elevation should be zero, and there is no roll as everything is axis-aligned.

camera_view.view_notes: Both grids fill the image left and right, axis-aligned, with flat silhouettes and no perspective; central whitespace is preserved by moderate zoom and left-right placement.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match original grid placement, alignment, and silhouette; both grids and the arrow are separated and preserved in scale
inline_constraints.visible_ui: show only 1 compact toggle (‘Show line pairs’) near the lower edge between grids; no filled panel
inline_constraints.explainers: use hover/click highlights for point correspondence and popup for line overlays
inline_constraints.framing: preserve original whitespace between grids and at figure borders
inline_constraints.camera: use orthographic, flat, straight-on view matching source crop and silhouette

interactionPlan.notes: Each grid cell and intersection should be interactive for hover feedback, but all highlighting must be subtle and not overpower the grid lines. The arrow between grids must be visually preserved but not interactive. For dragGridCorner, limit handles to only the four corners of the right grid to demonstrate user-controlled homography; movements interpolate the transform for the whole grid in real time. No color or legend overlays in the default state.

source_relations: The left grid is upright and viewed front-on (orthogonal projection), with all verticals and horizontals parallel.
The right grid is a quadrilateral, viewed at an angle giving a skewed perspective—top closer, bottom farther, and right edge retreats in depth.
A thick black arrow points horizontally from the center of the left grid to the center of the right grid.
The right grid's left edge is roughly aligned vertically with the left grid's right edge.
Both grids are nearly vertically centered in the figure.
rendered_relations: The left grid is upright and viewed front-on (orthogonal projection), with all verticals and horizontals parallel.
The right grid is a quadrilateral, viewed at an angle giving a skewed perspective—top closer, bottom farther, and right edge retreats in depth.
A thick black arrow points horizontally from the center of the left grid to the center of the right grid.
The right grid's left edge is roughly aligned vertically with the left grid's right edge.
Both grids are nearly vertically centered in the figure.

shape_comparison.source_shape: skewed quadrilateral grid (parallelogram-like, but with lines preserved as straight)
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: skewed quadrilateral grid (lines preserved as straight)

shape_comparison.source_shape: solid black arrow with straight shaft and triangular head
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: solid black arrow with straight shaft and triangular head

discrepancies: Right grid has small black circular handle dots at the corners, not present in the source image.
Checkbox UI label ('Show line pairs') appears at the bottom left, not present in the source figure.
Black grid line thickness is slightly heavier in the rendering than in the source.
Slightly less white space above and below the grids in the output—output is more tightly cropped vertically.
Visible handle dots on the right grid slightly change the minimalism of the target figure.
evaluation.notes: Very faithful grid geometry, correct 2D-to-3D mapping; slightly heavier lines and UI controls are present but all structure and relations are correct.
action_items: Remove or hide the draggable handle dots and interaction overlays in the initial rendering for a perfect static drop-in replacement.
Hide or fade the 'Show line pairs' control on load (unless toggled), as the base figure has no UI.
Reduce line thickness for a closer match to the original's graphic weight.
Slightly increase vertical whitespace to match the taller crop of the source figure.

feedback.rationale: All conceptual elements are accurately captured, but minor visual additions (handle dots, UI control) and styling mismatches require fixes at the generation level—no plan revision is needed.

Slightly increase vertical whitespace to match the taller crop of the source figure.
